name: Hypnos (Sleep)
shortDescription: Hypnos is the Greek god of sleep, son of Nyx (Night) and brother of Thanatos (Death). He resides in a cave near the River Lethe, where sleep-inducing vapors rise.
description: Hypnos is the Greek god of sleep, a gentle and benevolent deity who brings rest and tranquility to mortals and gods alike. He is the son of Nyx (Night) and Erebus (Darkness), and the twin brother of Thanatos (Death). Hypnos dwells in a dark cave near the River Lethe, the river of forgetfulness, from which soothing vapors emanate, inducing sleep. He is often depicted as a winged youth, sometimes nude, or as an old man with a beard, carrying a poppy stalk or a horn filled with sleep-inducing drugs. His primary role is to lull beings to sleep, offering respite from the burdens and anxieties of life. In Homer's *Iliad*, Hypnos plays a crucial role in aiding Hera's plan to deceive Zeus. Hera implores Hypnos to lull Zeus to sleep, allowing her to interfere in the Trojan War. Hypnos is hesitant due to a previous encounter where he angered Zeus, but ultimately agrees after Hera offers him Pasithea, one of the Graces, as his wife. This episode highlights Hypnos's power and influence, even over the king of the gods. Hypnos is generally considered a peaceful and benevolent god, although his power can also be used for manipulation or deception. His association with the River Lethe and forgetfulness further emphasizes the role of sleep in providing a temporary escape from reality and consciousness. He is a vital part of the cosmic order, ensuring the balance between wakefulness and rest.
